title: Broadband AC Magnetic Field Sensing via Continuous wave optically detected magnetic resonance with NV Centers in diamond
authors: Ryohei Dokai, Ryusei Okaniwa, Miku Ishizaki, Junko Ishi-Hayase, Yuichiro Matsuzaki
abstract: The nitrogen-vacancy (NV) center in diamond has attracted considerable attention as a highly sensitive quantum sensor that can operate at room temperature. In particular, continuous-wave optically detected magnetic resonance (CW-ODMR) is promising for a wide range of applications because of its simplicity. However, conventional AC magnetic-field sensing schemes based on CW-ODMR suffer from a limited detection bandwidth: the detectable frequency is either fixed by intrinsic physical parameters of the NV center or, even when tunable, restricted to a narrow range of only a few MHz. Here, we propose a broadband AC magnetometry scheme based on CW-ODMR with NV centers using microwave-driven dressed states.Through theoretical analysis and numerical simulations, we show that the proposed scheme enables the detection of AC magnetic fields with frequencies up to the order of 100 MHz, which has been difficult to achieve using conventional CW-ODMR-based methods.
summary: The authors propose a novel broadband AC magnetometry scheme utilizing NV centers in diamond and Continuous Wave Optically Detected Magnetic Resonance (CW-ODMR). By employing microwave-driven dressed states, they theoretically and numerically demonstrate the ability to detect magnetic fields up to 100 MHz. This overcomes the severe bandwidth restrictions of conventional CW-ODMR methods.
main_problem: Conventional CW-ODMR AC magnetic field sensing suffers from a severely limited detection bandwidth, typically restricted to only a few MHz.
main_result: The proposed scheme using microwave-driven dressed states enables the detection of AC magnetic fields up to the order of 100 MHz, significantly expanding the operational range.
method: Theoretical analysis and numerical simulations were used to model the system dynamics governed by a Lindblad master equation under microwave driving.
model_or_system: The physical system is the Nitrogen-Vacancy (NV) center in diamond. The dynamics are modeled using a Hamiltonian ($\hat{H}$) and solved via the Lindblad master equation, incorporating optical pumping transitions.
key_observables: Fluorescence intensity drop (dip) measured as the time-averaged occupation probability of the $|0angle$ state ($P_0$).
important_parameters: The achievable bandwidth is determined by tuning the Rabi frequency ($\lambda_D$), which can reach $\sim 100$ MHz.
main_assumptions: Approximations include assuming ideal projective measurement initially, and later accounting for measurement noise; the Rotating-Wave Approximation (RWA) is used but verified up to high frequencies.
figures_summary: Figures demonstrate dips in $P_0$ at predicted resonance frequencies, show that increasing the control field strength ($\lambda_D$) broadens the dip spacing toward 100 MHz, and quantify the achievable sensitivity ($30 \mu	ext{T}/\sqrt{	ext{Hz}}$).
paper_structure: The paper progresses from identifying the bandwidth limitation of conventional CW-ODMR to proposing a microwave-driven dressed state scheme. It details the theoretical Hamiltonian and master equation, derives the resonance condition ($\omega_T = \sqrt{2E \pm \lambda_D^2}$), and validates the broadband capability through numerical simulations.
why_interesting: This work directly addresses open quantum systems dynamics in a solid-state platform (NV centers) by engineering the system Hamiltonian via microwave driving to overcome inherent physical bandwidth limitations, which is highly relevant for quantum sensing applications.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
